elements.sent-2411-001: Direct preference learning offers a promising and computation-efficient beyond supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for improving code generation in coding large language models (LMs).
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the scarcity of reliable preference data is a bottleneck for the performance of direct preference learning to improve the coding accuracy of code LMs.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we introduce Direct Preference Learning with Only Self-Generated Tests and Code (DSTC), a framework that leverages only self-generated code snippets and tests to construct reliable preference pairs such that direct preference learning can improve LM coding accuracy without external annotations.
elements.sent-2411-004: DSTC combines a minimax selection process and test-code concatenation to improve preference pair quality, reducing the influence of incorrect self-generated tests and enhancing model performance without the need for costly reward models.
elements.sent-2411-005: When applied with direct preference learning methods such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO), DSTC yields stable improvements in coding accuracy (pass@1 score) across diverse coding benchmarks, including HumanEval, MBPP, and BigCodeBench, demonstrating both its effectiveness and scalability for models of various sizes.
elements.sent-2411-006: This approach autonomously enhances code generation accuracy across LLMs of varying sizes, reducing reliance on expensive annotated coding datasets.
elements.sent-2411-007: The recent advancement of large language models (LLMs) has significantly transformed numerous fields, including code generation.
elements.sent-2411-008: LLMs pre-trained on vast code corpora demonstrate substantial potential in generating code, and many studies have further enhanced these code models through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to improve performance on coding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Although SFT has shown promising results in improving code LMs, SFT focuses on replicating patterns within the dataset and often lacks the generalization needed to perform well on unseen instructions.
elements.sent-2411-010: This limitation has driven researchers to apply reinforcement learning (RL) to improve code LMs beyond the SFT stage by allowing them to adapt to more complex or variable inputs.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, one of the main challenges in applying RL to code LMs is the scarcity of high-quality preference datasets tailored specifically to code generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-012: Unlike standard language tasks, where user or human annotator feedback is widely available, a preference dataset for the code generation has unique requirements, including correctness and functional alignment, which complicates data collection and is much more expensive.
elements.sent-2411-013: Recent works, such as Starcoder2-15b-instruct \citep{wei2024selfcodealign , leverage execution feedback from the compiler to create SFT training datasets from self-generated tests and code snippets, aiming to simulate preference signals without human annotations.}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, this reliance on self-generated tests introduces risks: inaccuracies in these synthetic tests may result in unreliable annotations, which can negatively impact the effectiveness of the RL training process.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, in our experiments using the self-generated oss-instruct-sd2-50k dataset created by Starcoder2-15b-instruct, only 48\% of responses are correct when evaluated with an external model, Deepseek-coder-v2-instruct (with 236 billion parameters), highlighting a significant failure rate that suggests the need for a more reliable approach to leverage these synthetic tests effectively.
elements.sent-2411-016: In addition, direct preference learning methods, such as Direct Policy Optimization (DPO, \cite{rafailov2024direct ) and Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO, \cite{ethayarajh2024kto ), offer advantages in terms of computational efficiency and simplicity compared to more complex RL methods like PPO \citep{schulman2017proximal as they bypass the training of a reward model and a critic model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, we select the direct preference learning method as the backbone of the RL training framework and analyze the following problem in this paper: \begin{center With only self-generated code and tests, how can we generate reliable preference pairs to improve code generation accuracy for code LMs through direct preference learning? \end{center To address this question, we introduce a novel approach called Direct Preference Learning with Only Self-Generated Tests and Code (DSTC), which leverages only self-generated code snippets and tests to construct effective preference pairs.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: DSTC, as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:illus , comprises two main components: (1) a minimax selection mechanism and (2) a concatenation of code snippets and tests.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This framework aims to achieve two goals: (a) to enhance the quality of the chosen code-test pairs while enlarging the quality gap between the chosen and rejected code-test pair, and (b) to create more reliable preference pairs by training on concatenated code-test pairs that contain reliable binary execution feedback.
elements.sent-2411-020: By structuring the learning process in this way, DSTC reduces the noise from incorrect self-generated tests and establishes a more robust framework for preference learning in code generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-021: By incorporating both DPO and KTO, we implement DSTC on the model Starcoder2-15b-instruct with 15 billion parameters and evaluate the trained model on various benchmarks involving HumanEval Base \citep{chen2021evaluating , HumanEval Plus \citep{liu2024your , Mostly Basic Python Problems (MBPP) Base \citep{austin2021program , MBPP Plus \citep{liu2024your , and BigCodeBench (BCB) \citep{zhuo2024bigcodebench .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our results indicate that DSTC effectively increases the coding accuracy across multiple benchmarks, paving the way for designing an autonomous system to improve the ability of LM code generation.
elements.sent-2411-023: We also perform ablation studies to show the importance of each component in DSTC and demonstrate that DSTC can also improve the performance of the model Deepseekcoder-33b with 33 billion parameters.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/code_plot.pdf} \caption{Illustration of our proposed mechanism DSTC to reduce the misleading effect of the low-quality test and generate a more reliable preference dataset.} \label{fig:illus}
elements.sent-2411-024: We summarize our contributions in two-folds.
elements.sent-2411-025: We propose a mechanism DSTC to generate the preference pair from the self-generated code and tests to perform direct preference learning algorithms like DPO and KTO.
elements.sent-2411-026: DSTC consists of a minimax selection procedure and concatenation of the code and test.
elements.sent-2411-027: As a result, DSTC constructs preference pairs with a reliable correct order and enlarges the quality gap between the chosen code and rejected code, which reduces the misleading effect of incorrect self-generated tests and aids the training of direct preference learning methods such as DPO and KTO.
elements.sent-2411-028: Experimental results demonstrate that combining our proposed DSTC mechanism with DPO and KTO improves coding accuracy (pass@1 score) in code LMs across multiple benchmarks and model sizes (15 billion and 33 billion parameters).
elements.sent-2411-029: Ablation studies further validate the importance of each component within DSTC.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our work builds on a rich body of research on Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), a framework that has become essential for training state-of-the-art large language models (LMs) such as ChatGPT \citep{achiam2023gpt , Gemini \citep{team2023gemini , and Claude \citep{claude .}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the RLHF pipeline, LMs are fine-tuned to optimize a learned reward model using the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm \citep{schulman2017proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, PPO is often criticized for its instability, sample inefficiency, and sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning \citep{engstrom2020implementation , making it computationally expensive and less accessible for the open-source community.}
elements.sent-2411-033: To address these limitations, recent research in RLHF has explored alternatives to PPO-based methods, with direct preference learning algorithms emerging as a promising direction \citep{zhao2023slic, rafailov2024direct .}
elements.sent-2411-034: These approaches bypass the reward model training phase, aligning LMs directly with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-035: Since the introduction of the original Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) algorithm \citep{rafailov2024direct , numerous variants of the direct preference learning framework have been proposed, each tackling specific challenges from different angles \citep{liu2023statistical, azar2023general, xiong2023gibbs, tang2024generalized, ji2024towards, ye2024theoretical, pal2024smaug, hong2024orpo, rosset2024direct, liang2024robust, zhang2024negative, zhang2024self, zhang2024reward, xu2024just, tajwar2024preference, wu2024self, liu2024provably .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: One notable example is Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO), proposed by \citet{ethayarajh2024kto , which leverages the Kahneman-Tversky model of human utility to minimize a human-aware loss, in contrast to the standard Bradley-Terry model.}
elements.sent-2411-037: While these methods prioritize improving LM general alignment abilities with human preferences, our work focuses on a different and specific goal: enhancing pass@1 scores in code generation.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Large Language Models for Code Generation.
elements.sent-2411-038: Besides the great achievements in Natural Language Processing, LMs exhibit great potential in several complex reasoning tasks like math \citep{ahn2024large and coding \citep{jiang2024survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, pre-training on code datasets has resulted in impressive performance from models such as StarCoder \citep{lozhkov2024starcoder , DeepSeekCoder \citep{zhu2024deepseek .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Other models, like CodeQwen \citep{hui2024qwen2 and CodeLlama \citep{roziere2023code , have further enhanced their capabilities through ongoing pre-training with additional code data.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: To further improve the coding performance, there exists a rich line of literature that study how to fine-tune the current pre-trained LMs for coding.
elements.sent-2411-042: Among them, \cite{wei2024magicoder propose a mechanism to enlighten LMs with open-source code snippets to generate diverse instruction and \cite{luo2023wizardcoder adapt the Evol-Instruct method in the model fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: As another line of research to improve coding ability via RL, \cite{le2022coderl first introduce the RL framework in the training of LMs for code generation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This RL framework is refined by PPOCoder \citep{shojaee2023execution that incorporates the PPO algorithm in the training and \cite{liu2023rltf refine PPOCoder with a fine-grained feedback and a Critic Sampling mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: To tackle the exploration issue, \cite{dou2024stepcoder propose StepCoder by decomposing the long sequences code generation task.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our work aligns with emerging research that applies Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO) to train LMs using synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-047: For instance, \citet{gee2024code design a method to generate preference pairs from self-generated code based on correctness and efficacy, while \citet{zhang2024plum explore how KTO can improve coding ability with tests generated by ChatGPT.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: In contrast to these methods, our approach does not rely on ground-truth tests or correct solutions, which are expensive and difficult to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-049: Instead, our method leverages the current model without intervention from stronger models like ChatGPT.
elements.sent-2411-050: Furthermore, our method improves the pass@1 score after the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) phase by integrating direct preference learning methods such as DPO and KTO, which are more computationally efficient and stable than PPO-based approaches.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback and Direct Policy Optimization
elements.sent-2411-051: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) utilizes human preferences to guide the training of language models (LMs).
elements.sent-2411-052: A common approach involves pairwise preference modeling, where feedback is provided by comparing two model-generated responses to the same prompt.
elements.sent-2411-053: Consider a language model represented as a policy $\pi_\theta(\cdot|\cdot)$, parameterized by $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Given a prompt $x$ from the state space $\mathcal{X}$, the model generates a response $a$ from the distribution $\pi_\theta(\cdot|x)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: For a specific prompt $x$, the model generates two candidate responses $a^1$ and $a^2$ from the action space $\mathcal{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Human evaluators, or a reward model approximating human preferences, provide feedback as a binary label $y \in \{0, 1\}$, indicating whether $a^1 \succ a^2$ ($y = 1$) or $a^2 \succ a^1$ ($y = 0$).
elements.sent-2411-057: This preference is modeled probabilistically using the Bradley-Terry (BT) model \citep{bradley1952rank as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbb{P}_r(b = 1 | x, a^1, a^2) = \sigma(r(x, a^1) - r(x, a^2)), \label{eq:bt}\end{equation} where $r(x, a)$ is the human-provided score reflecting the quality of response $a$ for prompt $x$, and $\sigma(z) = 1/(1 + \exp(-z))$ is the sigmoid function.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}P_r(b = 1 | x, a^1, a^2) = \sigma(r(x, a^1) - r(x, a^2)), \label{eq:bt}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: The goal of RLHF is to learn a policy $\pi_\theta$ that maximizes the KL-regularized expected reward: \begin{equation}\max_{\theta} E_{x \sim \cD, a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \bigl[ r(x, a) - \beta \cdot KL(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) | \pi_ref(\cdot \mid x)) \bigr], \label{eq:rlhf}\end{equation} where $\pi_\text{ref}$ is the reference policy (e.g., the initial SFT model), $\beta$ is a hyperparameter, and $\cD$ represents the dataset.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\max_{\theta} E_{x \sim \cD, a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \bigl[ r(x, a) - \beta \cdot KL(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) | \pi_ref(\cdot \mid x)) \bigr], \label{eq:rlhf}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: Standard RLHF methods to solve \eqref{eq:rlhf , such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO, \citep{schulman2017proximal ), employ a two-stage process: first, a reward model is trained on preference data, and then the policy model is fine-tuned using the reward model.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Recently, direct preference learning algorithms have emerged to address the RLHF problem \eqref{eq:rlhf without training a reward model.}
elements.sent-2411-061: As the most popular one, Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) \citep{rafailov2024direct leverages \eqref{eq:bt and \eqref{eq:rlhf to reparameterize the policy through the reward model, enabling direct training of the policy model on preference data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Given the preference dataset \(\mathcal{D _\text{DPO = \{(x_i, a^+_i, a^-_i)\}_{i=1 ^N\) of $N$ preference pairs, DPO loss has the following closed form \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{DPO}}\left(\theta\right)=-\mathbb{E}_{(x, a^+,a^-)\sim \mathcal{D}_\text{DPO}}\left[\log\sigma(r_\theta(x, a^+)-r_\theta(x, a^-))\right], \label{reg_loss}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}r_\theta(x, a) =\log \frac{\pi_\theta(a \mid x)}{\pi_{\text {ref }}(a \mid x)}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Instead of using the BT preference model \eqref{eq:bt as DPO, \cite{ethayarajh2024kto proposed another direct preference learning algorithm Kahneman-Tversky optimization (KTO) from the view of Kahneman \& Tversky’s prospect theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In KTO, the preference dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{KTO = \{(x_i,a_i,b_i)\}_{i=1 ^N$ contains $N$ pairs of a prompt $x_i$ and a response $a_i$, accompanied by a binary variable $b_i$ that indicates whether $a_i$ is desired.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Given the preference dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{KTO }$ and the reference policy $\pi_\text{ref}$, KTO loss is defined as \begin{equation}L_{KTO}\left(\theta\right)=E_{(x, a, b ) \sim D_{KTO}}\left[\lambda_y-v(x, a)\right],\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} v(x, a) =\left\{\begin{array}{l} \lambda_D \sigma\left(\beta\left(r_\theta(x, a)-z_0\right)\right) \text {, if } b = 1 \lambda_U \sigma\left(\beta\left(z_0-r_\theta(x, a)\right)\right) \text {, otherwise } \end{array}\right. r_\theta(x, a) =\log \frac{\pi_\theta(a \mid x)}{\pi_{\text {ref }}(a \mid x)} z_0 =\mathbb{E}_{(x^\prime, a^\prime, b^\prime ) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{KTO}}}\left[\operatorname{KL}\left(\pi_\theta\left(a^{\prime} \mid x\right) \| \pi_{\text {ref }}\left(a^{\prime} \mid x\right)\right) \right]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Compared with DPO, we remark that KTO allows an imbalance between the number of chosen and rejected responses.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} v(x, a) =\left\{{l} \lambda_D \sigma\left(\beta\left(r_\theta(x, a)-z_0\right)\right) \text {, if } b = 1 \lambda_U \sigma\left(\beta\left(z_0-r_\theta(x, a)\right)\right) \text {, otherwise } \right. r_\theta(x, a) =\log \frac{\pi_\theta(a \mid x)}{\pi_{\text {ref }}(a \mid x)} z_0 =E_{(x^\prime, a^\prime, b^\prime ) \sim D_{KTO}}\left[\operatorname{KL}\left(\pi_\theta\left(a^{\prime} \mid x\right) \| \pi_{\text {ref }}\left(a^{\prime} \mid x\right)\right) \right]. }\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Large Language Models for Code Generation
elements.sent-2411-066: We formulate the code generation problem in the context of RLHF, where state $x\in\mathcal{X}$ refers to some natural language instruction and action $a\in\mathcal{A}$ refers to the response provided by the code LM $\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)$ that contains a code snippet $y\in\mathcal{Y}$.
elements.sent-2411-067: To check the correctness of a code snippet, one common approach is to feed the concatenation of the code snippet $y$ and a test $z$ to a compiler and output the binary execution feedback.
elements.sent-2411-068: Here, test $z\in\mathcal{Z}$ can be either hand-crafted or generated by LMs, which can also be incomplete or even wrong.
elements.sent-2411-069: Since we can execute the concatenation of code and test in a compiler and receive the binary feedback (pass or fail), we define a binary reward function $r(z,y):\mathcal{Z}\times\mathcal{Y}\mapsto\{0,1\}$ for any code snippet $y$ and test $z$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Assume that there exists a ground-truth test $z^\star_x$ for the instruction $x$ such that $z^\star_x$ can perfectly check the correctness of any code snippet for the instruction $x$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Similar to the RLHF objective in \eqref{eq:rlhf , the objective of code generation task can be formulated as \begin{equation}\max_{\theta} E_{x \sim \cD, a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \bigl[ r(z^\star_x, Ex(a)) - \beta \cdot KL(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) | \pi_ref(\cdot \mid x)) \bigr], \label{eq:code_rlhf}\end{equation} where $Ex$ function extracts the code snippet $y$ from the response $a$.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\max_{\theta} E_{x \sim \cD, a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \bigl[ r(z^\star_x, Ex(a)) - \beta \cdot KL(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) | \pi_ref(\cdot \mid x)) \bigr], \label{eq:code_rlhf}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: The similarity between \eqref{eq:rlhf and \eqref{eq:code_rlhf motivates us to leverage the direct preference learning method like DPO and KTO to solve the code generation task while bypassing the training of a reward function.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In this section, we introduce a novel mechanism called Direct Preference Learning with Only Self-Generated Tests and Code (DSTC), detailed in Algorithm \ref{alg1 and illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:illus .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: DSTC begins with prompting the model to generate multiple code snippets and tests for each given instruction.
elements.sent-2411-075: Given self-generated code snippets and tests, DSTC comprises two key components: minimax selection and code-test concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-076: Intuitively, DSTC extracts more reliable preference pairs from self-generated code snippets and tests while mitigating the misleading effects of incorrect tests or code.
elements.sent-2411-077: Using the preference dataset constructed by DSTC, direct preference learning algorithms such as DPO and KTO can be effectively employed to train code LMs.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: Preprocess: Generating code snippets and tests.
elements.sent-2411-078: Here, we adapt the prompt template\footnote{\url{https://github.com/bigcode-project/starcoder2-self-align from the oss-instruct-sd2-50k dataset \citep{wei2024selfcodealign to generate high-quality code and tests.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We show the details of the prompt in Appendix \ref{app:prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-080: To mitigate errors caused by mismatched entry points (e.g., function or class names) during the evaluation from a compiler, such as when assessing the correctness of a code snippet $y_i^{j_1}$ with a test $z_i^{j_2}$ where $j_1 \neq j_2$, we include entry points in the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-081: These entry points ensure the consistency.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Direct Preference Learning with Only Self-Generated Tests and Code (DSTC)} \label{alg1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Instruction set $\mathcal{I}=\{x_i\}_{i=1}^I$, sampling time $J$, model $\pi$, pre-defined prompt prompt for concatenation, and a compiler for returning binary execution feedback. \STATE Initialize the preference dataset $\mathcal{D}=\emptyset$. \label{line:0_start} \FOR{$i=1,\ldots, I$} \FOR{$j=1,\ldots, J$} \STATE Prompt the model $\pi$ to sample the code $y_{i}^{j}$ and test $z_i^j$ given the instruction $x_i$. \ENDFOR \label{line:0_end} \FOR{$j=1,\ldots, J$}\label{line:1_start} \FOR{$k=1,\ldots, J$} \STATE Concatenate the code $y_i^j$ and test $z_i^k$ to execute in the sandbox and receive the binary feedback $r_{ijk}\in\{0,1\}$. \ENDFOR \STATE Select $j^\prime = \argmax_j \sum_{k=1}^J r_{ijk} $. (Select the chosen code) \label{l:1} \STATE Select $k^\prime = \argmin_k \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad\text{ s.t. } r_{ij^\prime k} = 1$. (Select the chosen test) \label{l:2} \STATE Select $k^\dagger = \argmax_k \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad\text{ s.t. } \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} < J $. (Select the rejected test) \label{l:3} \STATE Select $j^\dagger = \argmin_j \sum_{k=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad\text{ s.t. } r_{ij k^\dagger} = 0$. (Select the rejected code) \label{l:4} \ENDFOR\label{line:1_end} \STATE Option I (DPO): \label{line:2_start} \IF{$j^\dagger$, $k^\dagger$, and $k^\prime$ are all not None } \STATE Concatenate code $y_i^{j^\prime}$, pre-defined prompt prompt, and test $z_i^{k^\prime}$ to get the chosen response $a_{i}^{\text{chosen}} = Concat(y_i^{j^\prime}, prompt,z_i^{k^\prime})$. \STATE Concatenate code $y_i^{j^\dagger}$, pre-defined prompt prompt, and test $z_i^{k^\dagger}$ to get the rejected response $a_{i}^{\text{rejected}} = Concat(y_i^{j^\dagger}, prompt,z_i^{k^\dagger})$. \STATE Add $(x_i, a_{i}^{\text{chosen}}, a_{i}^{\text{rejected}})$ in $\mathcal{D}$. \ENDIF \STATE Option II (KTO): \IF{$k^\prime$ is not None } \STATE Concatenate code $y_i^{j^\prime}$, pre-defined prompt prompt, and test $z_i^{k^\prime}$ to get the chosen response $a_{i}^{\text{chosen}} = Concat(y_i^{j^\prime}, prompt,z_i^{k^\prime})$. \STATE Add $(x_i, a_{i}^{\text{chosen}}, 1)$ in $\mathcal{D}$. \IF{$j^\dagger$ and $k^\dagger$ are both not None} \STATE Concatenate code $y_i^{j^\dagger}$, pre-defined prompt prompt, and test $z_i^{k^\dagger}$ to get the rejected response $a_{i}^{\text{rejected}} = Concat(y_i^{j^\dagger}, prompt,z_i^{k^\dagger})$. \STATE Add $(x_i, a_{i}^{\text{rejected}}, 0)$ \ENDIF \ENDIF \ENDFOR\label{line:2_end} \STATE Output: Preference dataset $\cD$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-082: After the preprocessing phase, the entry points are removed to prevent potential information leakage that could lead to overfitting during training.
elements.sent-2411-083: This feedback determines which code snippets and tests are chosen or rejected based on the following criteria (if an optimization problem is infeasible, the solution defaults to None):.
elements.sent-2411-084: Selection of the chosen code snippet.
elements.sent-2411-085: By measuring the correctness via the number of passing tests, we formulate the selection of the chosen code snippet $y_i^{j^\prime}$ as the following optimization \begin{equation}j^\prime = \argmax_j \sum_{k=1}^J r_{ijk}.\label{eq:1}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: We formulate the selection of the chosen test $z_i^{k^\prime}$ as the following constraint optimization \begin{equation}k^\prime = \argmin_k \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad s.t.  r_{ij^\prime k} = 1, \label{eq:2}\end{equation} where the constraint ensures that the chosen code snippet passes the chosen test.
elements.sent-2411-089: In contrast to \eqref{eq:2 , we formulate the selection of the rejected test $z_i^{k^\dagger}$ as the following constraint optimization \begin{equation}k^\dagger = \argmax_k \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad s.t.  \sum_{j=1}^J r_{ijk} < J,\end{equation} where the constraint ensures that at least one code snippet fails the rejected test.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Selection of the rejected code snippet.
elements.sent-2411-091: In contrast to \eqref{eq:1 , we formulate the selection of the rejected code snippet $y_i^{k^\dagger}$ as the following constraint optimization \begin{equation}j^\dagger = \argmin_j \sum_{k=1}^J r_{ijk} \quad s.t.  r_{ij k^\dagger} = 0,\end{equation} where the constraint ensures that the failed code snippet fails the rejected test.}
elements.paragraph-2411-006: Component II: Concatenation between the code snippet and test.
elements.sent-2411-092: Depending on which specific preference learning algorithm we use, we consider different manners to construct the preference dataset.
elements.sent-2411-093: For DPO, the preference dataset involves chosen-rejected pairs, hence we filter out the data without both feasible chosen and rejected responses generated via the minimax selection.
elements.sent-2411-094: For KTO, we only filter out the data without feasible chosen responses, as KTO preference dataset permits an imbalance between the number of chosen and rejected responses.
elements.sent-2411-095: With the preference dataset generated by DSTC, we incorporate the corresponding direct preference learning algorithm to train the code LMs.
elements.figure-2411-002: [H] 1\textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{ c c c c} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Accuracy} \multirow{2}{*}{Chosen Code $>$ Rejected Code (\%)} Chosen Code (\%) Rejected Code (\%) Baseline 45.1 23.1 26.2 DSTC 51.9 16.7 36.9 \end{tabular} } \caption{Results on the preference dataset generated by Starcoder2-15B-instruct, where Chosen Code $>$ Rejected Code means that the chosen code snippet passes the test but the rejected code snippet fails. We use DeepSeek-Coder-V2 with 236 billion parameters to generate the tests for evaluation.} \label{tab:analysis}
elements.sent-2411-096: In Table \ref{tab:analysis , we show that our proposed method DSTC can improve the code quality and enlarge the quality gap between the chosen code and the rejected code, making the preference pair more reliable.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Since there are still 36.9\% pairs of chosen code and rejected code in DTSC that they both fail or pass the corresponding tests, it outlines the necessity to incorporate the code-test concatenation to generate a more reliable preference dataset.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this section, we conduct extensive experiments to show the effectiveness of our proposed DSTC in improving LMs' code generation ability.
elements.sent-2411-099: We implement DSTC on two both open-resource fine-tuned models StarCoder2-15b-Instruct\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/bigcode/starcoder2-15b-instruct-v0.1 and Deepseek-coder-33b-instruct\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/deepseek-coder-33b-instruct , respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: We use the instructions from the oss-instruct-sd2-50k dataset\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/bigcode/self-oss-instruct-sc2-exec-filter-50k to form the instruction set, where all the instructions are generated and filtered by Starcoder2-15b given seed functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, StarCoder2-15b-Instruct is supervised fine-tuned (SFT) on the oss-instruct-sd2-50k dataset without further RLHF training.
elements.sent-2411-102: We use the implementation of DPO and KTO in the OpenRLHF codebase\footnote{\url{https://github.com/OpenRLHF/OpenRLHF \citep{hu2024openrlhf as our training framework.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The detailed training configurations are provided in Appendix \ref{app:detail_train .}
elements.sent-2411-104: We evaluate the LLM coding generation ability by five benchmarks: HumanEval Base \citep{chen2021evaluating , HumanEval Plus \citep{liu2024your , Mostly Basic Python Problems (MBPP) Base \citep{austin2021program , MBPP Plus \citep{liu2024your , and BigCodeBench (BCB) \citep{zhuo2024bigcodebench .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In particular, BCB consists of two splits: instruct split and complete split, where instruct split only involves NLP instructions and complete split uses structured docstring in prompts.
elements.sent-2411-106: For each split, hard subset in BCB contains the most challenging and user-centric fraction of the full set.
elements.sent-2411-107: During the evaluation of all these benchmarks, we use greedy decoding in the code generation and all results are reported as the pass@1 score.
elements.sent-2411-108: Here, the pass@1 score measures the accuracy of code LMs on their initial attempt at generating the correct code.
elements.subsection-2411-007: DSTC Enhances Code LMs Across Diverse Benchmarks
elements.sent-2411-109: The results in Table \ref{tab:benchmark1 demonstrate that DSTC consistently enhances the performance of code LMs, surpassing baseline models by a stable margin across all benchmarks when combined with either DPO or KTO.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Notably, DSTC achieves improvements in the pass@1 score without requiring additional resources, such as canonical solutions or synthetic tests generated by ChatGPT.
elements.sent-2411-111: This highlights its potential for developing automated systems to further advance code LMs effectively.
elements.figure-2411-003: 1\textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{ c c cccc ccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{2}{c}{HumanEval} \multicolumn{2}{c}{MBPP} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Complete)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Instruct)} Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Ref. 72.0 62.2 75.2 60.9 14.9 45.1 12.2 37.6 DSTC + DPO 74.4 66.5 76.4 61.9 16.2 47.5 12.8 37.9 DPO 70.1 62.2 76.9 62.2 14.2 46.6 12.2 38.1 DSTC + KTO 73.8 66.5 75.9 61.4 16.9 47.1 14.2 38.9 KTO 62.8 54.3 74.9 61.2 15.5 46.6 8.1 38.1 \end{tabular} } \caption{Evaluation results of DSTC incorporated with DPO Dand KTO on Starcoder2-15b-instruct.} \label{tab:benchmark1}
elements.paragraph-2411-008: DSTC can improve code LM with a larger size.
elements.sent-2411-112: Evaluation results in Table \ref{tab:benchmark2 showcase the effectiveness of DSTC when applied to the DeepseekCoder-33b-instruct, a large-scale code language model with 33 billion parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-113: These findings underscore the scalability of DSTC, demonstrating its ability to improve performance even in substantially larger models.
elements.figure-2411-004: [H] 1\textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{ c c cccc ccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{2}{c}{HumanEval} \multicolumn{2}{c}{MBPP} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Complete)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Instruct)} Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Ref. 77.4 70.1 81.7 69.0 19.6 50.9 14.9 42.0 DSTC 79.9 72.0 82.5 70.4 22.3 51.6 18.2 {41.0} \end{tabular} } \caption{Evaluation results of DSTC incorporated with DPO on DeepseekCoder-33b-instruct.} \label{tab:benchmark2}
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Both components in DSTC are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-114: To study the effect of each component in DSTC, we perform an ablation study on DSTC and report the results in Table \ref{tab:benchmark3 , which shows that removing any single component leads to a measurable decline in performance.}
elements.sent-2411-115: These results highlight the integral role of each component of DSTC in boosting the model's ability to generate accurate and reliable code.
elements.figure-2411-005: [H] 1\textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{ c c cccc ccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{2}{c}{HumanEval} \multicolumn{2}{c}{MBPP} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Complete)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{BCB (Instruct)} Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Basic (\%) Plus (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Hard (\%) Full (\%) Ref. 72.0 62.2 75.2 60.9 14.9 45.1 12.2 37.6 DSTC 74.4 66.5 76.4 61.9 16.2 47.5 {12.8} 37.9 DSTC w/o Concatenation 72.0 65.2 76.7 62.2 15.5 47.0 12.8 38.4 DSTC w/o Minimax Selection 71.3 62.2 75.4 61.4 15.5 46.5 14.2 37.5 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study of DSTC incorporated with DPO on Starcoder2-15b-instruct.} \label{tab:benchmark3}
elements.sent-2411-116: In this paper, we propose a novel mechanism named DSTC to incorporate direct preference learning methods such as DPO and KTO to enhance the code generation capabilities of LMs using only self-generated code snippets and tests to create preference pairs, circumventing the need for costly annotated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-117: By employing a minimax selection process and concatenated test-code pairs, DSTC mitigates the impact of low-quality self-generated tests, enabling stable performance gains across multiple coding benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-118: Applied with direct preference learning methods like DPO and KTO, DSTC consistently improves pass@1 scores, showing both scalability and generalizability for models of different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-119: This work highlights DSTC’s potential to advance coding accuracy autonomously, marking a step forward in resource-efficient, high-performance code LM training.
elements.sent-2411-120: Future directions may explore additional mechanisms for refining self-generated tests and expanding DSTC’s applicability across broader coding tasks.. \bibliographystyle{ims .}
elements.sent-2411-121: We train all the models with 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs and the OpenRLHF codebase \citep{hu2024openrlhf .}
elements.sent-2411-122: We report the hyperparameters for the training in Table \ref{tab:hype .}
elements.figure-2411-006: [H] 1\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c |cc|c} Base models \multicolumn{2}{c}{Starcoder2-15b-instruct} Deepseek-coder-33b-instruct Preference learning KTO DPO DPO Learning rate 5.0e-7 5.0e-7 5.0e-7 Learning scheduler type cosine\_with\_min\_lr cosine\_with\_min\_lr cosine\_with\_min\_lr Batch size 512 256 256 Micro batch size 4 4 1 Training epoch 1 1 1 $\beta$ 0.3 0.2 0.2 Optimizer adamw adamw adamw Precision bfloat16 bfloat16 bfloat16 Warmup ratio 0.05 0.05 0.05 \end{tabular} } \caption{Training configurations for all the models in this paper.} \label{tab:hype}
elements.section-2411-007: Prompts for Generating Code Snippets and Tests
elements.sent-2411-123: In this section, we give details of the prompts used to generate code snippets and tests.
elements.sent-2411-124: We show an example of the full prompt as follows.
elements.sent-2411-125: From a pool of 10 high-quality examples \citep{wei2024selfcodealign , we select one example to serve as the context for the generation.}
elements.sent-2411-126: As discussed in Section \ref{sec:method , we include the entry point in the instruction (i.e., The main function name is format\_tags.) to prevent compiler errors caused by mismatched entry points.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Additionally, we conclude the prompt with the directive sentence We will organize our response into four parts: [Reasoning], [Implementation], [Explanation], and [Tests].
elements.sent-2411-128: This sentence ensures the model follows the required format, facilitating the parsing of code snippets and tests.
elements.sent-2411-129: We train all the models with the OpenRLHF codebase \citep{hu2024openrlhf .}
